segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, slightly ominous orchestral score accompanying a daytime cityscape of a busy New York intersection, displaying '2009' in large white text. A male narrator begins, referencing "the 2008 housing crisis" as the scene transitions to a nighttime aerial view of Manhattan, showcasing illuminated streets and skyscrapers, including an iconic golden-tipped building. The camera then focuses on a darkened glass facade, revealing the 'J.P. Morgan Chase' logo as the narrator describes "the once-in-a-lifetime financial storm" and how "J.P. Morgan Chase" became "the largest bank in America" by surviving and capitalizing on the crisis. The scene shifts to a dimly lit auditorium where a man with wavy hair, dressed in a dark suit, speaks and gestures to an audience. A close-up reveals his face, with 'JAMIE DIMON' appearing in bold yellow letters, as the narrator identifies him as "the Wall Street banker, Jamie Dimon." The orchestral music swells as a framed portrait of the same man is shown, followed by another close-up of his face in a dimly lit setting. A blue curtain-like transition leads to the glowing blue 'FINAiUS' logo, accompanied by an ethereal sound effect and a female voice pronouncing "Finius." The music then shifts to an upbeat, percussive track with ambient city sounds as a bright, sunny day in Jackson Heights, NY, is shown, featuring pedestrians, cars, and a 'TACO' shop, with location text overlaid. The video concludes with a framed portrait of a younger Jamie Dimon with longer hair, displayed in a gallery setting, as the narrator states, "Jamie Dimon is born into the finance business."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with archival footage of a large ocean liner, reminiscent of the Titanic, navigating icy waters with tugboats, as a male narrator begins to recount a historical narrative, stating that "In 1921, his grandfather came to New York with nothing." This transitions to a bustling 1920s or 1930s city street scene, filled with pedestrians in period attire and vintage automobiles, while the narrator continues, describing how the grandfather "built a successful career as a banker. And then as a stockbroker." The scene shifts to a modern interior, where a framed photograph of a formally dressed couple is shown, as the narrator explains, "By the time Jamie's born, his father Theodore Diamond also has become a wealthy stockbroker." A close-up of a man in a dark suit speaking animatedly follows, with the on-screen text 'women's libbers' appearing briefly. He states, "Well, you know, my parents were real moralists, so you they, you know, you do the right thing, you only do the right thing, you treat people everyone equally and fairly. My mom was one of the earliest women's libbers around after reading, uh, you know, some of those books and stuff like that." The narrative then cuts to grainy, vintage home video footage of children playing outdoors near a wooden cabin and a serene lake, creating a personal, familial atmosphere, as the narrator describes "Diamond's childhood is almost too perfect, attending private schools and spending summers in the mountains in Colorado." This is followed by a series of framed black-and-white photographs of young boys, one with long hair, displayed on a wall, reinforcing a theme of personal history, while the narrator comments, "His life has been the envy of other children of the same age. But to the young Jamie, it isn't enough." The video concludes with a man in a white shirt, identified by on-screen text as 'Rocky Xu,' speaking expressively in a dimly lit room, possibly an office or studio, with a monitor visible in the background, as the narrator states, "From day one, Jamie has an unwavering ambition. He wants to make a fortune, more than that of his father and grandfather." Rocky Xu then speaks, explaining, "He recognized early on that to succeed achieving this goal, he should be in the finance industry." Throughout the segment, a consistent, somewhat dramatic, and reflective instrumental score plays in the background, underpinning the narrative.

The scene then shifts to Rocky Xu, a man in a white shirt, speaking expressively with hand gestures in a dimly lit room, as the narrator concludes that "Jamie Diamond feels he's entitled to go to the best school in America" and "decides the best way to prove he's worthy of greatness is by becoming the number one student at Tufts." Finally, Rocky Xu's voice takes over, describing how "Jamie Diamond always seemed to have this supreme confidence and sometimes comes off as cocky," as the final shot shows a framed photograph of the young man in a graduation gown, holding a paper, displayed on a wall, with Xu concluding, "His plan worked. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark suit and striped tie, speaking animatedly and gesturing in front of a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop. He recounts a past experience in a clear, articulate voice, stating, "I'd had a summer job there... he liked the fact I went to Harvard Business School... and his pitch was this: I'm not gonna pay you the same, but he said, come join me here... but at least you'll see what a big company works like." The scene transitions to a close-up of 'The REAL DEAL: MY LIFE in BUSINESS and PHILANTHROPY' by Sandy Weill, with his credit cards, as an upbeat musical score begins and a male narrator introduces him as "an accomplished dealmaker in the finance industry," who "changed the fate of many iconic Wall Street brands, like Primerica, Solomon Brothers, and Citibank." The visual shifts to a bright, sunny street scene in Jackson Heights, NY, with a 'TACO' shop, as the narrator details Weill's origins: "Born in a Jewish neighborhood in the Bensonhurst section of Brooklyn." A montage of black-and-white and sepia-toned photographs of a younger man in suits, military uniform, and office settings illustrates the narrator's description of Sandy Weill: "always wanted to get rich, not by working a nine-to-five job, but by creating his own company and leveraging other people's money." A Wall Street Journal front page from September 5, 1997, headlined 'American Express to Acquire Shearson!', visually reinforces the narrator's account: "By 1986, he sold his brokerage firm Shearson to American Express for $900 million. He becomes the president of American Express." A joyful color photograph of a man in a pink shirt laughing with others appears as the narrator mentions Weill taking "a liking to the young Jamie Dimon and offers to hire him as his personal assistant." The final shot shows a hand holding a New York Times article titled 'Deals in the Making', featuring a black-and-white photo of two men shaking hands, with 'Steve Wynn, RESORT & CASINO DEVELOPER' text on screen. The narrator's voice fades out saying, "Like most young people who get breaks...", which is then picked up and continued by a second male voice, "...who get breaks..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling broadly in a dimly lit setting, as a dramatic, suspenseful score begins. A narrator introduces the theme of high-stakes deal-making, mentioning "negotiating deals on behalf of while, worth hundreds of millions of dollars." The scene transitions to a close-up of Jamie Dimon, a man with wavy, graying hair in a dark suit and tie, standing against a city skyline, as the narrator states that "at the age of 28, Jamie Dimon already feels he's at the top of the finance game," but warns that "fate will bring him down to rock bottom." A whoosh sound accompanies a transition to a nighttime aerial view of a large suspension bridge, likely the Brooklyn Bridge, illuminated and bustling with traffic, establishing a New York City atmosphere. The narrator then shifts to the 1980s, describing it as "a decade of deal-making" under President Ronald Reagan, who "prioritized helping corporate America, cutting taxes and making it easy for them to borrow money," leading to "mergers and acquisitions, or simply, deals." A second, deeper male voice interjects, emphatically stating, "Only good deals." The visuals continue with a crowded indoor event featuring an American flag and clapping audience, followed by a man in a dark suit and red tie speaking earnestly. The scene then cuts to a dark, rainy urban street at night with a black car and pedestrians, creating a moody feel. Inside a modern setting, a man in a blue suit smokes a cigar, exuding authority. A blurred, fast-moving blue and black abstract shot serves as a transition. The narrator resumes, explaining that "after landing a job working directly for the president of American Express, Jamie Dimon's career skyrockets." A close-up of a man with dark, wavy hair looking intently is shown, followed by a group of men in suits in a serious boardroom discussion. The narrator concludes by noting that "Jamie Dimon knows, if he can help his mentor accomplish that, someday, American Express will also be his."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Rocky Xu, a man in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively and gesturing in a dimly lit room, narrating a corporate history. He explains in a clear voice how American Express, with its Fireman's Fund, saw Sandy Weill wanting to sell it to Warren Buffett, and how he and Jamie Dimon worked tirelessly on the deal, only for the board to veto it. A close-up of an older man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and a red patterned tie, looks seriously at the camera before the scene returns to Rocky Xu, who continues his commentary, stating Sandy Weill quit American Express in anger but encouraged Dimon to stay. Next, a close-up of a man with graying hair and a mustache in a dark suit appears against a blue background, his expression intense. The narrative then shifts to a framed photograph of a younger Jamie Dimon, smiling in a graduation gown, placed next to a red book titled 'WHO'S AFRAID OF IBM?'. An older man with graying hair, wearing a dark suit and a red-and-blue striped tie, is then seen standing at a podium with a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, speaking in a distinct, somewhat gravelly voice. He recounts his decision to leave in 1985 due to a difference in management style, noting that American Express stock tripled during the four years he was there. An exterior shot of the American Express Tower, a grand stone building with classical architecture, arched windows, and columns, its name clearly visible above the glass entrance, is shown. The man at the podium continues his speech, followed by a close-up of another man in a dark suit and red tie looking serious. Rocky Xu's voice returns, stating the 29-year-old Dimon also quit his job. The older man at the podium concludes, describing Jamie Dimon as a 'very loyal guy' who worked for him.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of Baltimore’s skyline, showcasing modern buildings and a harbor filled with boats, with the word 'BALTIMORE' prominently displayed. Upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as a male voice, Rocky Xu, starts narrating, explaining the search for a company from an "unlikely place." The scene transitions to a close-up of a tall building labeled 'Commercial Credit.' Rocky Xu continues, identifying Commercial Credit as "a loan company in Baltimore, targeting middle and lower-class consumers," and describing it as "pretty much a loan shark operation" lending high-interest, short-term loans. A distinct mechanical whirring sound accompanies a cut to a vintage 'CONTROL DATA | 6600' computer terminal with two green circular screens, as Rocky Xu reveals that Commercial Credit's parent company is "a computer disk drive maker, called Control Data," which he characterizes as "a bizarre acquisition, doomed to fail from the get-go." The visuals then show a close-up of a 'New York Times' article titled 'An Era Ends at Control Data,' detailing the company's struggles. Rocky Xu explains that by 1985, Control Data was "in financial trouble" and ready to sell its child company for cash. An older man is then seen from behind, intently reading a 'Form 10-K' document for 'Commercial Credit, Inc.' from 1988. Rocky Xu continues, stating that "Sandy Weill instructs Jamie Dimon to analyze the firm for a potential takeover," and Dimon "comes back with a shocking finding." As Rocky Xu describes Jamie Dimon as someone who "thinks differently," the text '4% ROE' appears on screen, directly correlating with Rocky Xu's subsequent statement that "Commercial Credit was a crappy company with just 4% return on equity," contrasting it with similar companies having 15%. The music continues throughout, maintaining an analytical and historical tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Rocky Xu, a man in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room, his clear, informative voice discussing a company's assets, costs, and attractive bargains. He narrates how Warren Buffett was approached for investment, explaining Buffett's shift from 'cigar butt companies' to 'quality companies,' influenced by Charlie Munger. This commentary plays over a close-up of 'The Snowball: Warren Buffett and the Business of Life' book by Alice Schroeder, placed beside a red Coca-Cola can. The scene then cuts to Warren Buffett, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red tie, seated and speaking during an interview. His distinct, slightly gravelly voice is heard reflecting on lessons from Ben Graham and the unpleasantness of scaling 'scraunchy and unpleasant' businesses that involved 'firing people.' Buffett is later seen in a different setting, wearing a striped tie and a gold watch, interacting with a smiling man in a blue suit. Rocky Xu's voice returns, stating Buffett rejected the offer, making an 'epic comeback' seem 'impossible.' Rocky continues, explaining 'three possible ways to take over this company,' including a wealthy co-investor like Buffett (which failed), and as he mentions the second way, a 'leverage buyout,' the large white text 'LBO' appears on screen. A subtle, dramatic background music underscores the entire informative and analytical narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a gritty urban scene featuring a weathered stone wall, a poster declaring 'YOU CAN'T HANDLE LEVERAGE!!', a red British postbox with an orange cone, and parked bicycles, as a male narrator discusses the risks of taking over a company by borrowing heavily, leading to a heavy debt burden and potential bankruptcy. He then introduces a third option: a public offering by selling ownership to the public, with the challenge of convincing investors. This transitions to Rocky Xu, in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with a monitor in the background, as the word 'IPO' appears, visually reinforcing the narrator's explanation of public offerings. The scene shifts to a dramatic close-up of Sandy Weill, a man with wavy, graying hair, intensely focused at a desk under a lamp, holding glasses and a pen amidst papers. A newspaper clipping titled 'Control Data Unit Gets Weill' details his appointment, while the narrator explains that Jamie Dimon sees commercial credit as an undervalued company, and Weill decided to put the majority of his own fortune, buying 10% of the company, and offering 80% to the public. The video then shows a smiling man in a dark suit at a formal event, with '$425K' overlaid, as the narrator reveals this is Jamie Dimon, who, despite being in his early 30s, invested $425,000 into the company. A black screen with 'THE IPO WAS A SUCCESS' appears, followed by a montage of stock trading scenes with '$850M' superimposed, coinciding with the narrator's announcement that they raised $850 million, instantly making their investments profitable. The segment concludes with two smiling men in suits, one with a mustache and the other with dark hair, implying a successful partnership, as the narrator adds that Sandy Weill appointed Dimon as the company's Chief Financial Officer and gave him a large number of shares. Throughout, a consistent, slightly dramatic background music underscores the narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of two smiling men in suits, one with graying hair and a mustache, as a male voice discusses taking a company public and raising enough money to secure an investment-grade credit rating. This transitions to an older man in academic regalia speaking at a podium, followed by an exterior shot of the 'Commercial Credit' building, accompanied by ambient city sounds and a building background score. A narrator then explains that after taking over Commercial Credit, costs were ruthlessly cut, including laying off 10% of employees. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vintage television set displaying chaotic scenes from a stock exchange floor, where traders in colorful jackets are seen in a frenzied state, pushing and shouting, as a male narrator describes the "1987 panic, crash, freefall, meltdown," noting how people "just put their hands in their pockets and stared in disbelief." The screen then transitions to a digital display showing the S&P 500 Index with a sharp decline, highlighting a dramatic market drop. Alan Greenspan, identified as the 'Former Chairman, Federal Reserve,' then speaks with a serious expression in warm, dim lighting, stating that "every crisis of this type, whether it's 2008 or 1873, has got the same roots, namely that it was unanticipated." The video returns to the vintage TV, now showing a close-up of a trader in a red jacket yelling, reinforcing the panic. A framed photograph of two smiling men in suits is shown, followed by an exterior shot of the grand 'Commercial Credit' building with green columns. A digital display beside its entrance shows 'Fortress Balance Sheet' with '1987' and images of the two men from the photograph, as the narrator explains that "while many of the biggest companies in the country struggled to survive, Weill and Diamond see an opportunity," believing that "with a strong balance sheet, Commercial Credit simply fitter than anybody else." The scene transitions to a close-up of a New York Times article dated September 20, 1992, titled 'Sandy Weill, Takeover Prince, Goes on the Prowl Again,' detailing his financial ambitions and acquisitions, with a black-and-white photo of him at his desk. The narrator continues, describing how "they start buying back their shares cheaply and taking over struggling firms next to nothing," becoming "the most prolific dealmakers" and even managing "to devour a bigger company, an insurance company called Primerica, with Jamie Diamond as the architect behind the deal."

An old-fashioned television then displays a C-SPAN2 broadcast of a 'FINANCIAL SERVICES BILL SIGNING CEREMONY' where President Bill Clinton is seen signing a document amidst clapping officials. A male narrator's voice introduces "Arthur Clinton" as a Democrat president who cut taxes on capital gains and real estate. As President Clinton signs the bill, the narrator explains how "Sandy Weill," described as the "apex predator of Wall Street," influenced the Clinton administration to create the Financial Service Modernization Act, enabling the merger of commercial banks and insurance companies, ushering in "the age of mega banks." The visual then cuts to a man speaking at a podium with a 'Citi' logo, whom the narrator identifies as "Jamie Dimon," Weill's "young and ambitious protege," made president of Citi Group. The scene shifts to a dimly lit, moody shot of a man in a dark sweater standing by a lamppost near a turbulent ocean, as the narrator explains that for Dimon, being president "is not enough"; he desires his mentor's seat. The video then shows a wall adorned with framed photographs, including a serious portrait of a man in a suit and a group photo of smiling men, while the narrator reveals that "Sandy Weill," as a "master dealmaker," always has a "plan B." The final segment features Rocky Xu, a man in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively with hand gestures in a dimly lit room, as the narrator concludes by describing Jamie Dimon as a "skilled and intelligent businessman" who is "often too blunt and has a big temper," traits that "can make him vulnerable."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of **Sandy Weill**, an older man in a dark suit and red tie, smiling warmly at a formal event, identified by the narrator as the CEO of Citigroup. The scene transitions to **Jessica Bibblovich**, a woman with blonde hair, wearing a plaid blazer and diamond earrings, laughing joyfully, who the narrator reveals is Sandy Weill's daughter, brought into the 'Diamonds division' as a backup plan. The narrator explains that Jessica and 'Jamie' are childhood friends, whose friendship is about to be tested, as suspenseful music builds. A series of framed photographs are displayed, including a formal couple and another smiling man and woman. The narrative continues, explaining that 'Diamond' initially welcomed Weill's daughter by promoting her to chairman of the company's mutual fund division. A man with graying hair is seen walking past a 'Vanguard' display, which the narrator connects to the rise of low-fee mutual funds that 'Diamond' believes will eventually prevail over high-cost ones. The narrator states that 'Diamond' trusts Jessica to restructure her business towards low-cost mutual funds, an idea Sandy Weill objects to, but 'Diamond' eventually prevails. The scene cuts to two men in suits conversing in front of a blue 'Citibank' backdrop, one gesturing expressively, with the narrator explicitly naming **Jamie Diamond** as the one who, a year later, denies Jessica a promotion, believing she didn't do a good job. A calendar featuring a photograph of Jessica in a green tweed jacket is shown, followed by an exterior shot of a 'Citi Smith Barney' building at 1200 Third Ave, over which a significant financial figure of '$288M' is displayed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, seated and gesturing expressively in front of a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop. He recounts in a clear, reflective voice how he "worked for Sandy for 14 years" but "didn't get along with him the last five," leading to him being fired. Somber background music underscores the narrative as the scene transitions to a black-and-white New York Times clipping, headlined 'Citigroup President, Heir Apparent to Top Job, Quits Abruptly,' featuring a photograph of a younger Jamie Dimon. An unseen narrator confirms that in November 1998, after 15 years with his mentor Sandy Weill, Jamie Dimon was indeed fired. Dimon's voice returns, describing the emotional moment he went home and told his wife, Judy, and their three young daughters, quoting one who asked, "Dad, do we have to sleep on the streets?" He reassures them, "No, hun, we're okay," amidst a montage of images including him speaking at a podium, a formal couple, and vintage photographs. He then reflects on the experience, stating, "when you fail at something, it's your... net worth, not myself worth," and that he "was able to move on," despite it being "difficult." The segment concludes with a dimly lit scene of a man with dark, wavy hair, wearing a light blue sweatshirt, sitting contemplatively on a couch, appearing distressed before clutching his chest, visually embodying the emotional turmoil as the narrator states Dimon was "one step away from becoming the most powerful man on Wall Street, but now he is unemployed."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a CNBC exclusive interview featuring Sanford Weill, former Citigroup Chairman & CEO, seated in a studio with a modern, brightly lit background of orange and blue geometric panels and real-time financial data on screen. Weill, in a dark pinstripe suit and red-and-blue striped tie, speaks with a serious expression, his voice reflecting on a past professional conflict: "He was doing very well, and he thought he was ready to run the company... But the only problem was I wasn't ready to retire. And then... we began to not cooperate." As dramatic, suspenseful orchestral music begins, a narrator's voice takes over, stating, "Jamie Dimon is no stranger to failure." The scene cuts to a man with graying hair in a dark coat speaking intensely in a dimly lit public space, followed by a close-up of a thoughtful man in a white shirt and dark tie looking upward. The narration continues, detailing how "Over the years of corporate conquest with Sandy Weill, Dimon has built a strong reputation as an effective corporate leader." Rocky Xu is then seen speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with a monitor, as the narrator adds, "Dimon believes he's robbed of his right to become the CEO of Citigroup." A man with wavy, graying hair in a dark sweater speaks reflectively in a shadowy room with a bookshelf, while the narrator emphasizes, "The best revenge is through massive success. Dimon will build an empire, bigger than Citigroup." The segment concludes with a wide shot of a man in a white shirt walking purposefully toward the entrance of a classical-style building labeled 'BANK ONE,' under bright daylight, as the narrator states, "And to do that, he will need to find a business as his launching pad... By 1999, to Dimon's surprise, a perfect opportunity lands at his doorstep."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling confidently as large white text '$50M' appears over him, while a calm, informative narrator reveals he has "all of his fortune in Bank One stocks, worth over $50 million." The scene cuts to a black screen with white text stating 'The stock goes up 30%,' indicating a positive market movement. Next, Nassim Nicholas Taleb, a man with a white beard and glasses, wearing a dark blazer, stands in front of a whiteboard with a graph, gesturing as he speaks in a clear, authoritative voice, explaining his "skin in the game" rule: "nobody should put others at risk without having harm to himself," emphasizing that if you lose money for clients, "you should be exposed to the same risk." A book cover titled 'SKIN IN THE GAME: Hidden Asymmetries in Daily Life' by Nassim Nicholas Taleb is shown, linking the lecture to the book’s themes. The narrator then introduces a figure named "Diamond" gambling his future on Bank One, which he may have "underestimated just how bad the company is." A man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking animatedly against a red-lit background with a slightly gravelly voice, describes how "Bank One was much tougher than JP Morgan," "failing," with "stocks down," and the need to "fix a company" by potentially laying off "a lot of people." The video transitions to an exterior shot of a classical-style building labeled 'BANK ONE,' with a man in a white shirt walking toward it, implying a purposeful visit by "Diamond." Finally, the same man in the dark suit and purple tie is seen in the back of a dark car, adjusting his tie and speaking, conveying a sense of urgency or contemplation as he travels through a cityscape, as the narrator details how "Diamond" will "sell underperformed businesses," "cut executive compensation," and "fire 12,000 employees to cut costs" to beat the company into shape.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, seated in a black leather chair against a muted green and gray wall. He speaks expressively, using hand gestures, his voice passionate and authoritative as he recounts taking away employee benefits like cars, clubs, 401K matches, and financial planning, stating, "because folks those are entitlements. We're going to lay off people. You're in good shape, they aren't. And we, collectively, are to blame. And I want you to stand forward and be proud that you're cutting your stuff first." The scene then transitions to a black-and-white filter displaying large white text overlays, first '$4.4B', then '-$511M'. A different male voice (Rocky Xu) begins to speak, explaining that "He took 4.4 billion dollars in write-downs and loan loss provisions in 2000. And the company ended up posting a loss of 511 million dollars for the year." The video cuts to Rocky Xu, a man in a light-colored shirt, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit room with a monitor, his hands moving as he analytically continues, "Wall Street is liking what Diamond's doing. The stock goes up because they finally see someone who's willing to do what it takes. Jamie Dimon has one objective: building a fortress balance sheet." This is followed by a shot of a book titled 'A FORTRESS FOR BANKING' by Jamie Dimon, placed on a desk. Rocky Xu's voice continues, "Because if your balance sheet is a fortress, a real one, not one that you've convinced yourself of..." The final scene features Frank Bisignano, CEO of Fiserv, standing in a dark suit and yellow tie, gesturing as he speaks in front of a dark backdrop, with on-screen text identifying him.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking expressively in a formal CNBC interview. His clear, confident voice explains, "we just reduced... reduced the balance sheet we were taking on. And we thought we're helping the system. We only did it because we asked to by Hank Paulson. And we thought if it went down, that could be a huge crisis for the globe." The scene transitions to archival footage of Dimon walking alongside another man outside a classical building, as a male narrator, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music, describes him as a "shrewd banker" offering "$2 a share." News footage of the Bear Stearns building appears, with ABC News overlays declaring 'DEVELOPING STORY: WALL STREET CRASH, FED ACTS TO STEM CRISIS,' and graphics showing its stock plummeting from $170 to $2.00 per share. An energetic news reporter's voice announces, "Bear Stearns, the Wall Street legend... is being sold to JP Morgan Chase for just $2 a share," noting that "last year Bear shares were selling for $170." The narrator clarifies that the initial offer was "too small" and the company was ultimately "sold to JPM at $10 per share." The camera pans across the Bear Stearns glass facade before cutting to the J.P. Morgan building at night, symbolizing the acquisition. The narrator explains that while the deal didn't benefit J.P. Morgan's bottom line, it "certified JP Morgan's reputation as a sound bank to do business with" and "sends Jamie Dimon to the very top of the financial industry." The sequence concludes with Dimon walking confidently through a bustling city street, followed by a close-up of two books, 'Last Man Standing: The Ascent of Jamie Dimon and JPMorgan Chase' and 'The House of Dimon: How JPMorgan’s Jamie Dimon Rose to the Top of the Financial World,' both featuring his image, underscoring his powerful rise. The narrator's voice continues, stating, "By the end of 2008, Jamie Dimon is a practical..." before the audio fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of two books, 'Last Man Standing' and 'The House of Dimon', both featuring Jamie Dimon, as a narrator declares him "the last man standing" and that "The House of Morgan is now the House of Dimon." The scene transitions to a sunlit New York City street, then a nighttime shot of a J.P. Morgan building at East 47th St and Madison Ave, followed by a wide cityscape featuring One World Trade Center, overlaid with '$200B' and '+27%'. The narrator explains that J.P. Morgan Chase was seen as safer, almost a bank of last resort, taking in $200 billion in money market management and increasing commercial banking income by 27% in 2008, while the rest of the country was gripped by recession. As the narrator notes the country's recession, the focus shifts to a Citi building, then a black-and-white newspaper article from November 6, 2007, detailing the 'Ouster of Citigroup Chief' Charles O. Prince III, with a photo of him looking contemplative, accompanied by a dramatic, low-frequency musical shift, signaling "a different story" for Citigroup. A color image of a serious Charles O. Prince III appears, followed by a close-up of a man with graying hair and a mustache speaking at a podium with a 'group' sign. The narrator explains that after Sandy Weill retired, Chuck Prince's leadership proved a mistake due to heavy exposure to troubled mortgage-backed securities, leading to the bank's early troubles in 2007. The board then removed Prince, replacing him with Vikram Pandit. The segment concludes with a black screen as the narrator somberly states that it was "already too late," the "damage is done," and Sandy Weill watched the "storm unfold" from retirement, with the dramatic music persisting.
